#Summary1=(human1) Author David Bollier discusses various examples of corporations over-extending their intellectual property rights to prevent their brands and products from being used in any uncontrolled way, drawing attention to what can be considered common knowledge by highlighting cases of Barbie, campfire songs, and even the prefix “Mc” being barred from use in everyday scenarios.

#Summary2=(claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929) David Bollier argues that intellectual property law has become excessively restrictive, citing examples of corporations using copyrights and trademarks to bully individuals and suppress free expression, thereby transforming legal protections intended to promote creativity into anti-social instruments of control that privatize common knowledge and cultural elements.

#Summary3=(gpt4o) David Bollier argues that the increasing trend of treating knowledge as private property, through excessive use of copyrights, trademarks, and patents, stifles creativity and free speech, citing examples like ASCAP's attempts to charge summer camps for singing and corporations aggressively defending trademarks against small entities and individuals.

#Summary4=(Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct) David Bollier argues that the concept of intellectual property has become a form of market fundamentalism, prioritizing corporate control over the free circulation of knowledge and creativity.

#Summary5=(Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) The document argues that the expansion of intellectual property rights is leading to a privatization of common knowledge and culture, with large corporations and entities using these rights to exert control over public expressions and meanings, often at the expense of free expression and public interest.
#Text=13 : The Plot to Privatize Common Knowledge ( Bollier )
#Text=By David Bollier
#Text=#reportinginformation #descriptive #sharedvalues #currentevents #research #pathos #logos #kairos #cognitivebias #artsandculture #intellectualproperty
#Text=“ adler barbie articulated ” by deborah is lola is licensed under CC BY - NC - SA 2.0
#Text=Over the past three decades , modern culture has become infatuated with the idea that knowledge should be owned like real estate or stock shares .
#Text=The original idea , of course , is that copyrights , trademarks and patents reward people for their creative labors and thereby boosts the common good .

#Text=But this line of thinking has come to resemble a kind of Market Fundamentalism : copyrights , trademarks and patents are the only morally legitimate and practical method for managing creations of the mind .
#Text=There is no middle ground .
#Text=You either believe in intellectual property rights , or you support “ theft ” and “ piracy .
#Text=This fundamentalist approach shuts down a broader discussion about how knowledge ought to circulate in our culture .
#Text=To avoid any confusion , let me just say straight - up that I believe in copyrights and patents .
#Text=In some cases , they provide significant and necessary incentives to invest in new works .

#Text=But today , copyrights and patents are going far beyond their intended goals — such as the U.S. Constitution provision to “ promote progress in science and the useful arts ” – to become ends in themselves .
#Text=Instead of carefully balancing private interests and public needs , copyrights and patents are becoming crude , anti-social instruments of control and avarice .

#Text=This is the conclusion that I came to in my book Brand Name Bullies , which is filled with dozens of stories of copyright and trademark owners bullying citizens , artists , scholars and others with ridiculous legal threats .
#Text=Silent Campfires

#Text=One of my favorite stories about the alarming expansion of copyright law involves ASCAP , the American Society of Composers , Authors and Publishers , the organization that collects performance licensing fees from public establishments where recorded music is played .
#Text=ASCAP decided that their domain should be extended to summer camps .
#Text=Why should n’t boys and girls singing around the campfire be considered a “ public performance ” that should pay royalties ?

#Text=A while back ASCAP approached the American Camping Association and said it wanted blanket performance licenses from hundreds of summer camps – something on the order of $ 300 to $ 1,400 per season per camp .
#Text=This caused quite a ruckus .
#Text=When it was discovered that ASCAP wanted money for the Girl Scouts to sing “ This Land Is Your Land ” and “ Puff , the Magic Dragon ” , the press went nuts .
#Text=There were stories about camps resorting to non-copyrighted songs like “ The Bow - Legged Chicken . ”
#Text=An ASCAP official heartlessly told a reporter : “ They [ camps ] buy paper , twine and glue for their crafts – they can pay for the music too . ”
#Text=Eventually , after a huge public outcry , ASCAP backed down .
#Text=But its claim to legal authority in charging summer camps for their “ public performances ” of copyrighted songs remains intact .
#Text=Lawsuit Barbie
#Text=The issue in so many of these battles is : Who shall control the “ public meaning ” of familiar images ?
#Text=Mattel is legendary in trying to protect the cultural “ meaning ” of Barbie .
#Text=It has gone after any unauthorized uses of Barbie .
#Text=It went after a series of photographs by Mark Napier called Distorted Barbie , which dared to depict Barbie as fat or as having Down ’s Syndrome .
#Text=Even highly distorted images of Barbie that were essentially unrecognizable were deemed unacceptable by Mattel .
#Text=Mattel went after a magazine that caters to adult collectors of Barbie dolls .
#Text=Mattel even pressured the Seattle publisher of a book , Adios , Barbie : Young Women Write About Body Image and Identity , to change the title .
#Text=The book was reprinted as Body Outlaws .

#Text=This extreme clampdown on free expression spurred culture - jammers , such as the self - styled Barbie Liberation Organization , which substituted voice boxes of GI Joe with those in Barbie , so that GI Joe would say , “ Let ’s plan our dream wedding , ” and Barbie would yell , “ Vengeance is mine ! ”
#Text=I am happy to report , a federal circuit court in the United States put a damper on Mattel ’s bullying litigation .
#Text=The case involved Utah photographer Tom Forsythe , who made a series of 78 photos of Barbie for his Food Chain Barbie exhibit .
#Text=It featured Barbie in enchiladas , stuffed into a blender and in other kitchen poses .
#Text=Only a few of Forsythe ’s photos sold .
#Text=He spent about $ 5,000 to mount the exhibit , and lost money .
#Text=No matter ; Mattel wanted to send a message that you ca n’t mess with Barbie .
#Text=It spent years litigating the case , requiring Forsythe to find pro bono legal counsel , which spent nearly $ 2 million defending him .
#Text=Forsythe prevailed in the circuit court , which delivered a stinging rebuke to Mattel for bringing a “ groundless and unreasonable ” trademark dilution claim .
#Text=Watch Your Words
#Text=The privatization of words — language is one of the most basic form of commons — is another disturbing trend .

#Text=The Japanese corporation that owns the “ Godzilla ” trademark has a habit of threatening all sorts of people who use the phoneme “ zilla , ” including a website called “ Davezilla ” that featured a lizard - like cartoon character .
#Text=The corporate obsession with owning words is really quite extensive .
#Text=McDonald ’s claims to own 131 words and phrases .
#Text=The San Diego - based McDonald ’s actually claims to own the Irish prefix “ Mc . ”
#Text=It has successfully prevented restaurant from naming their businesses McVegan , McSushi and McMunchies .

#Text=Ralph Lauren , the clothing line , went after Polo magazine , run by an equestrian organization , claiming it was a trademark infringement for the U.S. Polo Association to use the word “ polo ” on its line of clothing !
#Text=MasterCard went after Ralph Nader for using “ priceless ” in his campaign ads when running for President in 2000 .
#Text=( Nader ’s free speech rights ultimately prevailed . )

#Text=But the gay athletes who wanted to host a series of athletic competitions in San Francisco could not use the phrase “ Gay Olympics ” because that phrase is owned by the U.S. Olympic Committee , who gets to decide who can use it .
#Text=“ Special Olympics ” for disabled kids is OK , but not “ Gay Olympics . ”

#Text=The TV demagogue Bill O’Reilly reportedly went ballistic when he learned that the comedian ( and now senator ) Al Franken was using the words “ fair and balanced ” as a subtitle in his book that mocked various right - wing pundit , including him .
#Text=The federal court laughed Fox News ’ case out of court , and Franken won .
#Text=But pity the people who ca n’t afford to hire Floyd Abrams , a prominent First Amendment attorney , to represent them .
#Text=A woman from Los Angeles dared to name her neighborhood newspaper the Beechwood Voice .
#Text=She was threatened with legal action by the Village Voice , which claimed that use of the word “ voice ” as a newspaper name diluted its trademark .
